title: Chrome v20 Offline Password Decryption on Windows
tags: chrome, dpapi, app-bound-encryption, forensics, red-team, dfir, windows
content: Disclaimer: This article is intended exclusively for security research, authorized penetration testing, digital forensics and incident response (DFIR), and educational purposes. All techniques described are published in existing public security research. Do not apply these techniques against systems you do not own or have explicit written authorization to test. The authors assume no liability for misuse.     Chrome v20 Offline Password Decryption on Windows  Introduction  Google Chrome’s credential protection model has undergone a fundamental architectural shift. With Chrome 127 (released July 2024), Google introduced Application-Bound Encryption (ABE) — a mechanism that binds the browser’s encryption keys not just to the user account via DPAPI, but to the application identity itself through a privileged COM-based Elevation Service. This effectively broke the long-standing “dead box” offline forensic workflow that the security community had relied on for years.  This article walks through the full decryption architecture — from the legacy v10 DPAPI model to the new v20 multi-layer scheme — and documents the offline decryption workflow as far as it can be taken without a live system. It is structured for security researchers, red team operators, and DFIR practitioners who need to understand what artifacts are required, which stages can be performed offline, and where live-system interaction remains necessary.      1. Objectives  1.1 Why Offline Decryption Matters  Offline credential decryption — operating on acquired artifacts without a running Windows session — is a fundamental capability in both offensive and defensive security operations. Its value derives from what it does not require: no live system access, no process injection, no API calls that generate EDR telemetry, and no dependency on the target machine’s uptime.  Red teaming and penetration testing: Offline decryption allows operators to work on exfiltrated artifacts in a controlled lab environment. This sidesteps endpoint detection: no suspicious API calls to CryptUnprotectData, no LSASS memory access, no COM calls to the elevation service. The entire decryption pipeline runs on attacker-controlled infrastructure.  DFIR and forensic analysis: Dead-box forensics has historically been the gold standard: image the drive, extract the artifacts, reconstruct the timeline. App-Bound Encryption has partially broken this model for Chrome 127+ — but understanding exactly which stages remain possible offline is essential for triage planning. Forensic investigators need to know when to act before shutting down a system.  Endpoint monitoring and security research: Understanding the decryption workflow enables defenders to write detections around the specific Windows artifacts and API calls involved. Knowing what an attacker needs (SYSTEM masterkeys, user masterkeys, CNG KSP material) informs what to monitor and protect.    1.2 Notable Browser Credential Decryption Projects and Tools  The following open-source projects are referenced throughout this article or are directly relevant to the decryption workflows described:                 Tool       Language       Target       Notes                       dpapick3       Python       v10 DPAPI offline       Core Python library for offline DPAPI; Chrome example included                 impacket dpapi.py       Python       v10 DPAPI offline       Full DPAPI offline support via SAM/SYSTEM/SECURITY hives                 SharpDPAPI       C# (.NET)       v10 DPAPI offline/live       GhostPack; supports Chrome credential extraction                 pypykatz       Python       DPAPI offline       Python reimplementation of mimikatz DPAPI modules                 mimikatz       C       DPAPI live/offline       dpapi::chrome; requires live session or offline hives                 chrome_v80_password_offline       Python       v10 offline       Reference implementation for v10 offline decryption flow                 xaitax/Chrome-App-Bound-Encryption-Decryption       C++       v20 live       Reflective process hollowing; no admin required                 runassu/chrome_v20_decryption              v20 as SYSTEM       Reverse-engineered PostProcessData; documents XOR key derivation                 Cookie-Monster (Kingdom of the NOPs)       BOF/Python       v20 live       BOF injection + Python postprocessing; exports to CuddlePhish JSON                 fantasywastaken/Chrome-App-Bound-Decryption              v20       CNG API + LSASS impersonation approach               2. Chrome v10 Password Offline Decryption (Windows)  2.1 Browser Credential Storage  Chrome stores credentials in SQLite databases within the user’s profile directory:                 Artifact       Path       Data                       Local State       %LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Local State       Encrypted master key (os_crypt.encrypted_key)                 Login Data       %LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Login Data       Passwords (password_value field)                 Cookies       %LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Network\Cookies       Cookie values (encrypted_value field)                 Web Data       %LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Web Data       Payment methods           In the v10 model, os_crypt.encrypted_key in Local State holds a Base64-encoded blob prefixed with the literal string "DAPI". Stripping the prefix and Base64-decoding yields a raw DPAPI blob — the encrypted AES-256 master key used to protect all credential database entries.  2.2 DPAPI Protection  The Windows Data Protection API (DPAPI) ties secrets to a user’s Windows credentials. A master key file, derived from the user’s password, is stored at:  %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Protect\<SID>\<MasterKeyGUID>   The DPAPI blob inside encrypted_key references this master key by GUID. Decrypting the blob requires:    The master key file   Either the user’s logon password (to decrypt the master key), or a domain backup key if the machine was domain-joined   In offline scenarios, master key decryption is performed without the Windows DPAPI service — using the raw key material extracted from the hive files or profile artifacts.  2.3 Retrieving and Decrypting the Browser Master Key  Artifacts required:    Local State file from the user profile   Master key file(s) from %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Protect\<SID>\   One of:            User’s Windows logon password (for password-based master key decryption)       Domain DPAPI backup key (for domain-joined machines)       Pre-key from lsadump::dpapi on a live system           Offline decryption with dpapick3:  # <!-- INSERT: working code snippet demonstrating dpapick3 usage --> # Reference: https://github.com/mis-team/dpapick/blob/master/examples/chrome     2.4 Offline DPAPI Decryption Using DPAPICK3  DPAPICK3 is a Python 3 fork of the original dpapick library, implementing the full DPAPI offline decryption chain without Windows API dependencies. It supports:     Password-based master key decryption   Domain backup key decryption   Credential decryption via recovered master keys   Chrome-specific helper functions     2.5 Credential Decryption Flow  Once the AES master key is recovered from os_crypt.encrypted_key, individual credential database entries (password_value, encrypted_value) can be decrypted. Each entry uses AES-256-GCM with a per-entry IV prepended to the ciphertext.  Per-entry blob structure (v10): [prefix: "v10" (3 bytes)][IV (12 bytes)][ciphertext (variable)][GCM tag (16 bytes)]    Figure 1: v10_decryption_scheme.png Caption: Chrome v10 offline decryption workflow — from Local State extraction through DPAPI master key decryption to AES-GCM credential recovery.    2.6 References                                                   2.7 Limitations of v10 Offline Decryption and the v20 Transition  The v10 workflow is fully offline-capable when the user’s Windows password or domain backup key is available. However, several implementations still rely on live Windows components:     Key Storage Provider access: Some tools call live Windows CNG/CSP APIs rather than implementing the cryptographic operations in pure Python/C.   SYSTEM DPAPI key retrieval: Tools requiring SYSTEM-context DPAPI (for pre-v20 SYSTEM-encrypted blobs) may call live LSASS rather than extracting from the registry.   Live Windows APIs: Tools using CryptUnprotectData require a live Windows session and the correct user context.   The v10 model’s fundamental weakness was its reliance on user-context DPAPI exclusively: any process running as the target user could request decryption — including malware. Google recognized this and, with Chrome 127, introduced a new protection layer requiring SYSTEM-level privilege for the outer encryption layer, combined with path validation to restrict which executables can legitimately request decryption. This is the Application-Bound Encryption model described in Section 3.    3. Chrome v20 Offline Password Decryption  App-Bound Encryption was introduced in Chrome 127 (July 2024) and has been progressively hardened in subsequent releases. The “v20” prefix in encrypted credential entries (0x76 0x32 0x30) signals that a credential is protected under this new scheme.    3.1 Architectural Changes in v20  3.1.1 New Protection Model  The v20 protection model introduces two fundamental changes over v10:          Dual DPAPI encryption: The App-Bound key is encrypted first in user-context DPAPI, then wrapped again in SYSTEM-context DPAPI. A user-level process can no longer decrypt it without first obtaining the SYSTEM masterkey.           Path validation: The encrypted blob embeds the normalized path of the calling executable (chrome.exe). The Elevation Service enforces this at decryption time — a different executable requesting decryption will fail validation even if it has the correct DPAPI keys.      3.1.2 The Elevation Service  Chrome’s Elevation Service (elevation_service.exe) is a COM server that runs as SYSTEM and serves as the sole legitimate decryption endpoint. It exposes the IElevator COM interface:                 Detail       Value                       Chrome CLSID       {708860E0-F641-4611-8895-7D867DD3675B}                 Brave CLSID       {576B31AF-6369-4B6B-8560-E4B203A97A8B}                 Interface method       IElevator::DecryptData                 Runs as       SYSTEM           3.1.3 Application-Bound Encryption Concepts  The app_bound_encrypted_key stored in Local State (JSON key: os_crypt.app_bound_encrypted_key) is a Base64-encoded blob prefixed with "APPB". The plaintext structure before double-DPAPI encryption is:  [validation_data_length (4 bytes)][validation_data][app_bound_key_length (4 bytes)][app_bound_key (32 bytes)]   Where validation_data contains:    Normalized executable path (e.g., C:\Program Files\Google\Chrome\Application\chrome.exe)   ProtectionLevel enum value (currently PROTECTION_PATH_VALIDATION = 2)   3.1.4 Differences from v10                 Aspect       v10       v20                       Outer encryption       User-DPAPI       SYSTEM-DPAPI                 Inner encryption       None       User-DPAPI                 Path validation       None       Yes (normalized chrome.exe path)                 Decryption endpoint       Any user process       IElevator COM (SYSTEM)                 Offline feasibility       Full (with user key)       Partial (SYSTEM + user keys needed; CNG KSP may block)                 Chrome 137+ (domain)       N/A       Additional CNG KSP layer                 Chrome 137+ (non-domain)       N/A       Hardcoded ChaCha20-Poly1305 layer            Figure 2: v20_decryption_scheme.png Caption: Chrome v20 Application-Bound Encryption architecture — dual DPAPI layers, Elevation Service path validation, and Chrome 137+ PostProcessData additional encryption.    3.2 Elevation Service and Decryption Flow  The Elevation Service implements the following sequence when IElevator::DecryptData is invoked:     Receive ciphertext: Accept the app_bound_encrypted_key blob from the calling process.   SYSTEM-DPAPI decrypt: Decrypt the outer DPAPI layer using the SYSTEM account’s master key.   User-DPAPI decrypt: Impersonate the calling process’s user token; decrypt the inner DPAPI layer.   Unpack blob: Extract validation_data and app_bound_key from the plaintext structure.   Path validation: Normalize and compare the calling process’s executable path against the path stored in validation_data. Reject if mismatch.   PostProcessData (Chrome 137+): Apply an additional encryption layer (see Section 3.5).   Return key: Return the plaintext app_bound_key to the caller.     References:                                     3.3 Offline Decryption of Microsoft Key Storage Provider Data  For domain-joined hosts running Chrome 137+, the PostProcessData function adds an additional layer using the Microsoft CNG Key Storage Provider (KSP). The key involved is identified by the label "Google Chromekey1" within the machine’s CNG KSP.  3.3.1 Key Hierarchy  [app_bound_encrypted_key] (Local State)     |     v [SYSTEM-DPAPI layer]  ←── SYSTEM masterkey (Section 3.4)     |     v [User-DPAPI layer]    ←── User masterkey (standard DPAPI, Section 2.2)     |     v [PostProcessData — domain-joined Chrome 137+]     |     ├── [encrypted_aes_key] ──► CNG KSP: "Google Chromekey1"     |                          XOR with static value in elevation_service.exe     |     v [app_bound_key (AES-256)]     |     v [AES-256-GCM decrypt] ──► plaintext credentials   3.3.2 Relevant Artifacts                 Artifact       Location       Purpose                       Local State       %LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Local State       Contains app_bound_encrypted_key                 CNG KSP key store       %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Crypto\Keys\ (user) or %ProgramData%\Microsoft\Crypto\ (machine)       "Google Chromekey1" container                 elevation_service.exe       C:\Program Files\Google\Chrome\Application\<version>\elevation_service.exe       Contains static XOR value             3.3.3 Offline Processing Workflow    Figure placeholder: cng_ksp_key_hierarchy.png Caption: CNG Key Storage Provider key hierarchy for domain-joined Chrome 137+ hosts.  3.4 Retrieving SYSTEM DPAPI Material  Decrypting the outer DPAPI layer of app_bound_encrypted_key requires the SYSTEM account’s DPAPI master key. The SYSTEM master keys are stored at:  C:\Windows\System32\Microsoft\Protect\S-1-5-18\User\   These master key files are themselves encrypted using a value derived from the DPAPI_SYSTEM LSA secret, which is stored in the SECURITY registry hive.  3.4.1 Acquisition Methods  Registry extraction (offline): # Collect required hives from live system or Volume Shadow Copy: reg save HKLM\SYSTEM system.hiv reg save HKLM\SECURITY security.hiv # Then copy: C:\Windows\System32\Microsoft\Protect\S-1-5-18\User\     Volume Shadow Copy acquisition (offline): # List shadow copies: vssadmin list shadows  # Access via VSS path (example): \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy1\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy1\Windows\System32\config\SECURITY \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy1\Windows\System32\Microsoft\Protect\S-1-5-18\User\     SYSTEM and SECURITY hive collection:  Both hives plus the SYSTEM master key directory must be collected together as a set. The master key files reference the LSA secret by implicit derivation — both are needed.  Mimikatz-assisted extraction (live system):  # Dump DPAPI_SYSTEM LSA secret and decrypt SYSTEM masterkeys: mimikatz # privilege::debug mimikatz # token::elevate mimikatz # lsadump::secrets mimikatz # dpapi::masterkey /in:"C:\Windows\System32\Microsoft\Protect\S-1-5-18\User\<GUID>" /system        Operational note: Collecting the SYSTEM and SECURITY hives, along with the SYSTEM master key files, may be the only stage of the v20 offline decryption workflow that requires either a live Windows system or a pre-acquisition collection step. Plan accordingly — image or collect these artifacts before shutting down the target system.   References:                                                3.5 Decrypting the app_bound_encryption_key  With all required material collected, the decryption of app_bound_encrypted_key proceeds through the following stages:  3.5.1 Required Inputs                 Input       Source                       Local State JSON file       Chrome user profile                 SYSTEM DPAPI master key (plaintext)       Section 3.4                 User DPAPI master key file + user password or backup key       %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Protect\<SID>\                 (Chrome 137+, domain) CNG KSP key for "Google Chromekey1"       Machine CNG store                 (Chrome 137+, domain) XOR static value       elevation_service.exe binary                 (Chrome 137+, non-domain) Static ChaCha20-Poly1305 key       elevation_service.exe binary           3.5.2 Intermediate Artifacts  1. app_bound_encrypted_key (base64, from Local State)      → strip "APPB" prefix → base64-decode → raw_blob  2. raw_blob      → SYSTEM-DPAPI decrypt (using SYSTEM masterkey) → inner_blob  3. inner_blob      → User-DPAPI decrypt (using user masterkey) → raw_plaintext  4. raw_plaintext      → parse: [validation_data_length][validation_data][key_length][app_bound_key]      → extract app_bound_key (32 bytes)  5. app_bound_key (Chrome 137+ domain-joined)      → CNG KSP decrypt of encrypted_aes_key → aes_key      → XOR aes_key with static value → derived_key      → AES-GCM decrypt of wrapped app_bound_key → final_key  6. final_key (or app_bound_key for pre-137 / non-domain)      → AES-256-GCM decrypt each credential entry   3.5.3 Final Decryption Stages  The per-entry credential decryption uses the recovered key against each encrypted_value / password_value field in the SQLite databases:  v20 entry blob structure: [prefix: "v20" (3 bytes: 0x76 0x32 0x30)][IV (12 bytes)][ciphertext (variable)][GCM auth tag (16 bytes)]     References:                                                  4. Proof-of-Concept Demonstration    4.1 Environment    Figure placeholder: lab_environment_diagram.png Caption: Lab environment topology for PoC demonstration.  4.2 Collected Artifacts    4.3 Decryption Steps    Step 1: Extract DPAPI_SYSTEM LSA secret  # <!-- INSERT: impacket-secretsdump command -->   Step 2: Decrypt SYSTEM master key  # <!-- INSERT: dpapi.py or mimikatz command -->   Step 3: Decrypt user master key  # <!-- INSERT: command -->   Step 4: Decode and decrypt app_bound_encrypted_key  # <!-- INSERT: script or command pipeline -->   Steps 5–7: Parse and validate plaintext blob  # <!-- INSERT: parsing script or annotated output -->   Step 8 (Chrome 137+ domain only): PostProcessData decryption  # <!-- INSERT: CNG/XOR processing -->   Step 9: Credential entry decryption  # <!-- INSERT: AES-GCM decryption command/script -->     4.4 Validation    4.5 Results   –>  PoC Repository:     5. Conclusion  Chrome’s Application-Bound Encryption represents a meaningful security improvement over the purely user-context DPAPI model that protected credentials for a decade. By introducing a SYSTEM-level intermediary (the Elevation Service), dual-layer DPAPI encryption, and explicit path validation, Google has substantially raised the bar for credential theft — particularly for malware operating at user privilege levels. The result is that the v20 scheme resists the entire class of user-mode infostealer attacks that previously operated against Chrome with impunity.  For red teams and penetration testers, the implications are nuanced. Offline decryption of v20 credentials remains feasible — but only when the attacker has obtained Administrator-level access sufficient to collect SYSTEM DPAPI material from the registry hives. This is consistent with Google’s own documented threat model: ABE explicitly does not protect against attackers who have achieved Administrator-level privileges. What it does accomplish is eliminating the low-privilege, no-artifact-required exfiltration path that made browser infostealers so prevalent.  For DFIR practitioners, the news is a mixed picture. The “dead box” workflow — image the drive, extract credentials offline at leisure — no longer applies cleanly to Chrome 127+. The SYSTEM DPAPI master keys must be collected from the live registry or a Volume Shadow Copy before meaningful offline analysis is possible. This puts a premium on live triage and pre-shutdown acquisition, shifting investigative practice toward approaches that have long been standard for volatile data (RAM acquisition, live registry dumps) but were previously less critical for browser credential forensics.  Chrome 137’s introduction of the PostProcessData layer — whether via CNG KSP on domain-joined machines or a hardcoded symmetric key on standalone machines — adds further complexity. The standalone hardcoded-key path may remain fully offline-capable through binary analysis of elevation_service.exe. The domain-joined CNG KSP path, depending on whether the key is TPM-backed, may represent a hard boundary for offline analysis.  Future research directions include:    Determining the TPM-backing status of the "Google Chromekey1" CNG container in enterprise deployments.   Tracking defensive hardening in Chrome 138+ and beyond — Google has signaled continued investment in this protection model.   Developing DPAPI audit detection rules around legitimate vs. unauthorized CryptUnprotectData callers.   Exploring whether the padding oracle attack surface identified by CyberArk (C4, CVE-2025-34091) has been fully mitigated in patched versions.   Investigating parity across Chromium-based browsers (Edge, Brave, Vivaldi) which implement the same elevation service architecture with different CLSIDs.   The research published by SpecterOps, CyberArk, xaitax, runassu, and the broader community has documented the v20 landscape thoroughly enough for practitioners to reason about their operational constraints. The remaining frontier is Chrome 137+ on domain-joined machines — where the CNG KSP layer may represent the first genuinely offline-resistant component of the Chrome credential protection stack.    6. References            Andrew Gomez (SpecterOps), “Dough No! Revisiting Cookie Theft,” SpecterOps Blog, August 27, 2025. https://specterops.io/blog/2025/08/27/dough-no-revisiting-cookie-theft/           xaitax, “Chrome-App-Bound-Encryption-Decryption — RESEARCH.md,” GitHub, 2025. https://github.com/xaitax/Chrome-App-Bound-Encryption-Decryption/blob/main/docs/RESEARCH.md           CyberArk Research, “C4 Bomb: Blowing Up Chrome’s AppBound Cookie Encryption,” CyberArk Threat Research Blog, 2025. https://www.cyberark.com/resources/threat-research-blog/c4-bomb-blowing-up-chromes-appbound-cookie-encryption           ElcomSoft, “Browser Forensics in 2026: App-Bound Encryption and Live Triage,” ElcomSoft Blog, January 2026. https://blog.elcomsoft.com/2026/01/browser-forensics-in-2026-app-bound-encryption-and-live-triage/           Sergey Gordeychik et al., “dpapick3 — Python 3 DPAPI library,” GitHub. https://github.com/mis-team/dpapick           @kabutor, “chrome_v80_password_offline,” GitHub. https://github.com/kabutor/chrome_v80_password_offline           GhostPack, “SharpDPAPI,” GitHub. https://github.com/GhostPack/SharpDPAPI           @gentilkiwi, “mimikatz — dpapi::chrome,” GitHub. https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz           Fortra / SecureAuth, “impacket dpapi.py,” GitHub. https://github.com/fortra/impacket           @skelsec, “pypykatz,” GitHub. https://github.com/skelsec/pypykatz           @runassu, “chrome_v20_decryption,” GitHub.            @fantasywastaken, “Chrome-App-Bound-Decryption,” GitHub. https://github.com/fantasywastaken/Chrome-App-Bound-Decryption           @thewhiteh4t (Lohitya Pushkar), “How to read DPAPI encrypted Chrome credentials offline,” Blog, 2024. https://thewhiteh4t.github.io/blog/how-to-read-dpapi-offline/           OtterHacker, “Decrypt SYSTEM DPAPI,” GitHub Pages. https://otterhacker.github.io/Pentest/Techniques/DPAPI.html           CoreSecurity, “Reading DPAPI Encrypted Keys with Mimikatz,” CoreLabs Articles. https://www.coresecurity.com/core-labs/articles/reading-dpapi-encrypted-keys-mimikatz           Google Security Blog, “Protecting Chrome Cookies with App-Bound Encryption,” 2024.

title: Welcome to Jekyll!
content: You’ll find this post in your _posts directory. Go ahead and edit it and re-build the site to see your changes. You can rebuild the site in many different ways, but the most common way is to run jekyll serve, which launches a web server and auto-regenerates your site when a file is updated.  Jekyll requires blog post files to be named according to the following format:  YEAR-MONTH-DAY-title.MARKUP  Where YEAR is a four-digit number, MONTH and DAY are both two-digit numbers, and MARKUP is the file extension representing the format used in the file. After that, include the necessary front matter. Take a look at the source for this post to get an idea about how it works.  Jekyll also offers powerful support for code snippets:  def print_hi(name)   puts "Hi, #{name}" end print_hi('Tom') #=> prints 'Hi, Tom' to STDOUT.  Check out the Jekyll docs for more info on how to get the most out of Jekyll. File all bugs/feature requests at Jekyll’s GitHub repo. If you have questions, you can ask them on Jekyll Talk.

title: My First Post
content: Hello world! This is my first blog post.
